text:
MRI NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST — HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY PROTOCOL

EXAMINATION: MRI of the neck with gadolinium contrast, dedicated head and neck oncology protocol
CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old female with 3-month history of left-sided odynophagia and throat discomfort; palpable left neck mass. Biopsy-proven squamous cell carcinoma, p16-positive. Staging evaluation of oropharyngeal primary and regional neck nodes.
TECHNIQUE: Multi-sequence MRI of the neck performed at 3.0 Tesla. Pre- and post-contrast sequences obtained. Sequences include axial T1, axial and coronal T2 with fat saturation, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, and axial/coronal/sagittal T1 fat-saturated post-gadolinium sequences following administration of 0.1 mmol/kg gadobutrol IV contrast. Multiplanar reformats obtained. Imaging from the skull base through the thoracic inlet.

FINDINGS:

PRIMARY TUMOR — BASE OF TONGUE:
There is a heterogeneously T2-hyperintense, avidly enhancing soft tissue mass arising from the left base of tongue, measuring approximately 2.8 x 2.1 cm in maximal axial dimensions (craniocaudal extent approximately 2.4 cm). The mass demonstrates restricted diffusion on DWI (low ADC values in the range of 0.7-0.8 x 10^-3 mm2/s), consistent with high cellularity. The lesion is centered on the left glossotonsillar sulcus and posterior left base of tongue, with extension anterolaterally toward the left vallecula. The lesion does not extend to or involve the epiglottis, and the pre-epiglottic fat space is preserved. There is no radiographically evident involvement of the intrinsic musculature of the tongue body; the genioglossus and hyoglossus muscles appear intact without infiltrative signal abnormality beyond the confines of the oropharynx. The midline tongue musculature is uninvolved and the tumor does not cross the midline. The left tonsillar fossa demonstrates asymmetric fullness contiguous with the primary lesion. There is no involvement of the posterior pharyngeal wall or soft palate. The retropharyngeal space is uninvolved. No evidence of bone involvement of the mandible or hyoid.

EVALUATION FOR DEEP SPACE EXTENSION:
The left parapharyngeal space fat is preserved. The left masticator space is uninvolved. There is no encasement or displacement of the internal or external carotid arteries. The left carotid space neurovascular structures are intact, with the tumor separated from the carotid bifurcation by a small rim of soft tissue, without evidence of direct vascular involvement. No skull base involvement. Foramina of the skull base are intact.

CERVICAL LYMPH NODES:
Left neck: There is a single enlarged left level IIA lymph node measuring 2.5 x 2.0 cm in axial dimensions. This node demonstrates a rounded morphology with loss of the normal fatty hilum, central low T2 signal consistent with internal necrosis, and thick peripheral irregular enhancement. There is no discrete imaging evidence of extranodal extension (the nodal capsule appears intact and the adjacent fat planes are preserved, though equivocal at the medial aspect). The necrotic nodal morphology and avid peripheral enhancement are highly suspicious for metastatic involvement. A few additional left level II lymph nodes are at the upper limits of normal in size (short axis 8-9 mm) without necrosis or irregular enhancement; these likely represent reactive adenopathy. No left level III, IV, or V nodal enlargement is identified. No left retropharyngeal adenopathy.

Right neck: No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. A small right level II node measures up to 7 mm in short axis, with preserved fatty hilum, consistent with reactive change. No right retropharyngeal adenopathy.

Bilateral submandibular glands: Unremarkable.
Parotid glands: Symmetric, unremarkable bilaterally.
Thyroid gland: Homogeneous, no discrete nodules or masses.

SOFT TISSUES AND VASCULAR STRUCTURES:
No abnormal soft tissue mass or infiltration beyond the described primary lesion. Bilateral carotid arteries are patent without significant atherosclerotic change at this level of examination. No flow voids concerning for vascular invasion. Vertebral arteries imaged are symmetric. No retropharyngeal abscess or deep space infection.

BONY STRUCTURES:
Mandible: No lytic lesion, cortical destruction, or marrow signal abnormality to suggest bony involvement. Hyoid bone intact. Cervical spine: Mild degenerative changes at C5-6 with disc desiccation and minor posterior osteophyte formation, without cord compression or significant foraminal narrowing. No suspicious osseous lesion.

LARYNX AND HYPOPHARYNX:
Laryngeal framework is intact. Vocal cords appear symmetric. Aryepiglottic folds unremarkable. No supraglottic, glottic, or subglottic mass. Pyriform sinuses are symmetric without mucosal thickening or mass.

IMPRESSION:
1. Heterogeneously enhancing left base of tongue mass measuring approximately 2.8 x 2.1 cm, consistent with the biopsy-proven primary squamous cell carcinoma. The mass extends to the left vallecula without involving the epiglottis or crossing the midline; the pre-epiglottic space is preserved and the intrinsic tongue musculature is not infiltrated beyond the oropharynx.
2. Single enlarged, centrally necrotic left level IIA lymph node (2.5 x 2.0 cm) with morphology highly consistent with nodal metastasis; no convincing imaging evidence of extranodal extension, though the medial capsular margin is slightly indistinct and should be correlated clinically.
3. No contralateral or bilateral cervical nodal enlargement suspicious for metastatic involvement.
4. No evidence of carotid artery encasement, skull base erosion, or deep space invasion that would preclude definitive radiation-based management.
5. No radiographic evidence of distant disease within the imaged field.

Overall findings are consistent with a left oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma with single ipsilateral nodal involvement, as clinically suspected.
